items.mistake: Picking the model with the lowest training error after sweeping complexity
items.fix: Select the simplest setting whose validation (or CV) error is near the minimum — training error always favors higher complexity.
items.why: Training error ignores variance; the highest-complexity model almost always overfits finite samples.
items.example: Degree-15 polynomial hits train MSE 0.01 but val MSE 0.45; degree 5 has val MSE 0.12 — ship degree 5.

items.mistake: Using parameter count alone as the complexity measure
items.fix: Tune the hyperparameter you control (depth, degree, alpha) and plot validation curves; account for regularization and data limits.
items.why: Regularized high-degree polynomials or early-stopped large nets have lower effective capacity than raw parameter totals suggest.
items.example: Ridge on degree-10 poly with alpha=100 behaves like degree-2 on CV MSE despite thousands of coefficients.

items.mistake: Applying a complexity setting tuned on 1k rows to a 1M-row deployment without retuning
items.fix: Re-sweep complexity when data scale or noise changes; larger datasets often support higher capacity before val error rises.
items.why: Variance shrinks with n — the validation minimum moves rightward as sample size grows.
items.example: Depth-4 tree best at 1k rows; at 500k rows depth-12 wins on CV without overfitting.

items.mistake: Increasing complexity when both train and validation error are already high
items.fix: That is correct for underfitting — but if train error is already low and val is high, decrease complexity or regularize instead.
items.why: Complexity reduces bias and increases variance — wrong direction when variance is already the bottleneck.
items.example: Train acc 99%, val 70% with depth-25 tree — reduce depth or add min_samples_leaf, do not go to depth-40.
